title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: To comprehensively capture species-wide diversity
outcome: we sequenced 989 natural S. cerevisiae isolates using Oxford Nanopore technology (ONT) (Ref: 19,34) (Fig. 1a), achieving an average depth of 95× and an N50 of 19.1 kb (Fig. 1b, Supplementary Fig. 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: to maximize contiguity and completeness
outcome: A hybrid assembly pipeline was utilized (Supplementary Fig. 2 and Methods), yielding chromosome-scale assemblies for 1,015 isolates.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Assembly quality was assessed across several metrics. Contiguity matched the reference genome (Fig. 1d), with a median of 1.06 contigs per chromosome and 97.2% of the chromosomes assembled into a single contig (Extended Data Fig. 1a, Supplementary Table 2 and Supplementary Note 2). Assembly sizes ranged from 11.17 Mb to 12.95 Mb (mean = 11.90 ± 0.17 Mb) (Extended Data Fig. 1b). Accuracy, based on Illumina data and Merqury estimates, had an average Merqury quality value of 41.5 (Extended Data Fig. 1c), and completeness averaged 99.1% by BUSCO, closely matching the reference score (Ref: 37) of 99.4% (Extended Data Fig. 1d).
outcome: These results confirm that, although they do not always encompass the entire telomere-to-telomere sequence (Supplementary Note 2), the assemblies exhibit contiguity and completeness close to those of the reference genome, reaching near telomere-to-telomere status.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Systematic validation of 500 SVs based on the mapping of short-read sequencing data
outcome: confirmed sequence disruption in 95% of the SV calls (Supplementary Note 3)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Ty elements are found spanning over 50% of the SV sequence in 39% of PAVs (1,834 events), 20% of inversions (46 events) and 9% of CNVs (104 events) (Supplementary Table 3).
context: Transposable elements (TEs), particularly Ty elements, are major contributors to SVs in S. cerevisiae.
outcome: (N/A)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Extrapolating SV accumulation with increasing sample size, we estimated a total of 7,237 SVs
outcome: indicating that our dataset captures more than 90% of all SV events in the species (Fig. 2b)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: We also estimated species coverage, the proportion of redundant SVs recovered, which reached 99.5%
outcome: suggesting nearly complete representation of shared SVs (Extended Data Fig. 2 and Supplementary Table 4)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Their site frequency spectra resembled those of nonsense mutations (Ref: 19)
outcome: suggesting strong deleterious effects (Extended Data Fig. 3b)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: to assess structural heterozygosity in S. cerevisiae
outcome: we used haplotype-resolved assemblies from 396 heterozygous isolates

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: SVs in subtelomeric regions showed higher heterozygosity (Extended Data Fig. 4d)
outcome: consistent with the known structural variability of these regions

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Inversions and translocations exhibited higher heterozygosity than PAVs and CNVs (Extended Data Fig. 4e). SV length was correlated with heterozygosity (Spearman R = 0.55, P = 2.6 × 10−8; Extended Data Fig. 4f), with SVs greater than 30 kb in size showing a marked shift towards heterozygosity (78% heterozygous) compared with smaller SVs (less than 30 kb, 45% heterozygous).
outcome: This size effect helps explain the increased heterozygosity observed for typically larger SV classes such as inversions and translocations (Extended Data Fig. 4g).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: One notable exception is a well-described reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 8 and 16, which confers sulfite resistance through the overexpression of the SSU1 gene located near the breakpoint (Ref: 38) (Fig. 2e). Hotspots for PAVs, CNVs and inversions often mapped to TE-rich regions or SV-prone genes, such as FLO (Ref: 39,40), CUP1 (Ref: 41), and genes with tandem repeats, such as HPF1, SPA2 and NUM1 (Ref: 42,43). An inversion hotspot on chromosome 14 overlaps a 24-kb region flanked by inverted repeats, probably driven by recombination (Ref: 44).
outcome: These findings indicate that SV hotspots arise either from genome fragility, linked to Ty elements or repetitive sequences, or from adaptive pressures targeting specific genes.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: To distinguish these mechanisms
outcome: we examined the distribution of hotspot SVs across species clades with known ecological origins (Ref: 45)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Out of the 46 hotspots, 23 were evenly distributed across clades and localized to fragile regions (Supplementary Table 5), whereas the remaining 23 were clade-enriched
outcome: reflecting either population bottlenecks or local adaptation—for example, SVs associated with sulfite and copper resistance were enriched in wine isolates (Supplementary Table 5)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: To explore the relationship between population structure and SV diversity
outcome: we built separate phylogenies using SNP and SV genotypes (Supplementary Fig. 3)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Despite minor differences, the overall tree topology was conserved, with clades clustering consistently
outcome: indicating distinct SV signatures per clade

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: A similar trend in the Alpechín clade
outcome: probably reflects SNP inflation due to introgression

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: By contrast, domesticated clades (French dairy, beer and wine) showed an excess of SVs
outcome: suggesting that SVs contributed to rapid adaptation during domestication

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Overall, our assemblies enabled a near-complete view of SV diversity in S. cerevisiae
outcome: revealing a landscape shaped by both population structure and adaptive processes

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: The high proportion of core and soft core genes (73.9%)
outcome: indicates moderate gene content variation, consistent with a closed pangenome typical of many eukaryotes (Ref: 14,18,25,46-48)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: The genes captured by our population represent 99.5% of the species estimate (Fig. 3b and Supplementary Table 7)
outcome: demonstrating the high completeness of our defined gene-based pangenome

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Accessory genes are highly enriched in subtelomeric regions (two-sided Fisher’s exact test, odds ratio = 0.03, P < 2.2 × 10−16; Fig. 3c and Supplementary Table 8)
outcome: further reflecting the genomic variability of these regions

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: we found that core genes are more highly expressed than accessory genes (Supplementary Fig. 5)
outcome: consistent with findings in other species (Ref: 14,18,49,50)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Functional enrichment analyses
outcome: confirmed that core genes are involved in essential biological processes (Supplementary Fig. 6 and Supplementary Table 9)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: To investigate the origin of non-reference genes
outcome: we aligned novel gene sequences to a curated eukaryotic database (Supplementary Table 8 and Methods)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Among the 2,199 novel genes, 1,233 (56.1%) showed highest similarity to close Saccharomyces relatives
outcome: suggesting introgression

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: and 358 (16.3%) were most similar to non-Saccharomyces species
outcome: which is indicative of horizontal gene transfers (HGTs)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Another 516 genes (23.5%) showed low identity to reference homologues but aligned best to S. cerevisiae
outcome: suggesting rapid evolution, and were classified as fast-evolving genes

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: The remaining 92 genes (4.2%) lacked significant similarity
outcome: and may represent de novo gene birth (Ref: 51)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Clustering isolates by gene presence/absence reveals strong population stratification (Extended Data Fig. 7).
outcome: Gene content variation is structured by population.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Enrichment analyses showed widespread introgression across clades, with notably high levels in Alpechín, Mexican agave, and French Guiana isolates
outcome: confirming past hybridization events (Ref: 19,53,54) (Fig. 3f and Supplementary Table 10)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: HGTs were also identified in wine isolates, consistent with previous reports (Ref: 19,55), and partially shared with the Mixed Origins 1 clade
outcome: suggesting post-acquisition intraspecific gene flow.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: we identified seven introgressed MEL genes encoding alpha-galactosidase activity, allowing growth on melibiose (Supplementary Table 8).
outcome: some introgressed genes appear to confer novel traits.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: These genes, present in phylogenetically distant clades and closely related to homologues from Saccharomyces paradoxus and Saccharomyces mikatae
outcome: are likely to represent parallel acquisitions (Fig. 3g), contributing to convergent functional adaptations.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: Including SVs and indels alongside SNPs increased trait heritability estimates by 14.3% on average (0.36 versus 0.41; Supplementary Fig. 7 and Supplementary Table 11)
outcome: in line with earlier reports (Ref: 8,56)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: we identified 7,768 significant associations linking 3,717 traits to 4,564 QTL (Fig. 4a and Supplementary Table 12), with 3,471 SNP-QTL, 230 indel-QTL and 863 SV-QTL. This corresponds to 6.5%, 10.5%, and 19.8% of tested SNPs, indels and SVs, respectively (Fig. 4b)
outcome: revealing a strong enrichment of SV-QTL (two-sided Fisher’s exact tests with false discovery rate (FDR) correction, P = 6.6 × 10−161 and 4.9 × 10−20 versus SNPs and indels)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: SV-QTL are enriched in subtelomeric regions (Fig. 4a; two-sided Fisher’s exact test, P = 0 and 6.2 × 10−50 versus SNP-QTL and indel-QTL)
outcome: in line with their known genomic location (Fig. 2d)

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: One major SV-QTL hotspot involves a recombination-driven fusion of the ALD2 and ALD3 genes, associated with 66 expression and 30 growth traits. This SV arose independently multiple times, producing five alternate coding sequences (Extended Data Fig. 8) and is strongly enriched in Beer and French dairy isolates (two-sided Fisher’s exact tests with FDR correction, P = 7.06 × 10−30 and 7.45 × 10−18)
outcome: underlining a possible positive selection in these specific environments

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: indels are strongly enriched for local associations, with 54.5% of indel-QTL classified as local, compared to 28.8% for SNPs and 26.2% for SVs (Extended Data Fig. 10c; two-sided Fisher’s exact tests with FDR correction, P < 10−18).
outcome: This higher proportion of local QTL among indels likely contributes to their overall greater effect size relative to SNPs and SVs.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
subsection: Distinct phenotypic effects of SV types
main_content: Associated SVs constitute 20.9% of the total deletions, 19.2% of CNVs and 13.5% of insertions.
outcome: This finding indicates an enrichment of QTL in deletions and CNVs in comparison to insertions (two-sided Fisher’s exact test, P = 9.4 × 10−3 and 0.026, respectively).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
subsection: Distinct phenotypic effects of SV types
main_content: Deletion-QTL exhibit an average effect size of 4.1 × 10−2, which is 1.2-fold that of CNV-QTL (3.3 × 10−2; two-sided Mann–Whitney–Wilcoxon test, P = 3.5 × 10−4) and 2.2-fold that of insertion-QTL (1.9 × 10−2; two-sided Mann–Whitney–Wilcoxon test, P = 3.3 × 10−9) (Supplementary Fig. 9). In addition, associated deletions have a local effect in 25.0% of the cases, which is analogous to the 25.4% of local associations for CNVs but lower than the 47.7% of local associations for insertions (two-sided Fisher’s exact test, P value = 9.4 × 10−4).
outcome: Overall, these results reveal the limited phenotypic effect of insertions in comparisons to other SVs, as evidenced by a reduced fraction of QTL and a diminished effect size. Insertions appear to be constrained to their local effect and are less frequently acting in trans.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
subsection: Distinct phenotypic effects of SV types
main_content: Unlike SV-QTL, TE-related SV-QTL are never located within subtelomeric regions
outcome: which is expected given the scarcity of TEs in these regions (95.6% of all TE-related SVs are located outside subtelomeric regions).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
subsection: Distinct phenotypic effects of SV types
main_content: Among common SVs, 13.1% of the TE-related SVs were found to be associated with the variation of at least one trait, which is similar to the 13.6% of non-TE-related associated SVs (two-sided Fisher’s exact test, P value = 0.91). QTL involving TE and non-TE-related SVs exhibit an average effect size of 2.41 × 10−2 and 2.43 × 10−2, respectively, which represents minimal variation (two-sided Mann–Whitney–Wilcoxon test, P value = 0.026).
outcome: Overall, TE-related SVs exhibit a similar phenotypic effect than other SVs.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: We identified 4,444 QTL for molecular traits and 168 for organismal traits, averaging 0.9 and 1.7 QTL per trait, respectively (Fig. 5a).
outcome: This suggests that organismal traits are probably genetically more complex, involving a larger number of contributing loci (two-sided Mann–Whitney–Wilcoxon test, P = 1.3 × 10−8).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: SV-QTL make up 18.6% and 41.1% of the total QTL for molecular and organismal traits, respectively, both enriched relative to the 7.4% frequency of SVs among common variants (two-sided Fisher’s exact test, P = 2.4 × 10−118 and 2.6 × 10−33). However, the enrichment is stronger for organismal traits (5.6-fold) than for molecular traits (2.5-fold)
outcome: suggesting that SVs have a more prominent role in shaping complex organismal phenotypes (Fig. 5c and Supplementary Fig. 10).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: We identified 4,444 QTL for molecular traits and 168 for organismal traits, averaging 0.9 and 1.7 QTL per trait, respectively (Fig. 5a). By contrast, QTL for molecular traits showed significantly higher effect sizes on average (3.9 × 10−2 versus 2.7 × 10−2; two-sided Mann–Whitney-Wilcoxon test, P = 8.1 × 10−12) (Fig. 5b). SV-QTL make up 18.6% and 41.1% of the total QTL for molecular and organismal traits, respectively
outcome: These findings highlight distinct genetic architectures: organismal traits tend to involve more, weaker-effect variants that are likely to be spread across regulatory layers, whereas molecular traits are influenced by fewer but stronger-effect variants. The pronounced enrichment of SV-QTL in organismal traits supports the idea that large variants may have more persistent phenotypic effects across multiple regulatory layers.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: To facilitate SV genotyping in other S. cerevisiae collections and capture the full spectrum of structural diversity
outcome: we constructed a reference graph pangenome using 500 haplotypes, including the linear reference genome.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: A first graph, built from whole-genome alignments using Minigraph (Ref: 58), included only SVs and reached a length of 48.6 Mb, 4 times the size of the linear reference (Fig. 5d and Supplementary Table 13).
outcome: This expansion is mainly due to sequence redundancy, with 88% of bases aligning back to the reference (Supplementary Note 4), and the over-representation of Ty elements, which make up 52.2% of the graph length compared to 1.6% of the average genome (Supplementary Table 14).

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: After removing redundancy, the sequence content reached 11.9 Mb, with 2.5 Mb (21%) absent from the linear reference, matching estimates from the gene-based pangenome. A subset of these non-reference regions (267 kb)
outcome: is likely to correspond to introgressions from other Saccharomyces species.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: for comprehensive genotyping
outcome: We then constructed a more detailed graph using Minigraph-Cactus (Ref: 59), integrating both small variants and SVs

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: The graph captures 97.5% of low-frequency and 98.8% of common SNPs (Supplementary Table 15), and improves genotyping accuracy compared to a linear reference. Notably, it yields a 10% average increase in heritability estimates across 8,153 traits (Supplementary Note 4).
outcome: Together, these results highlight the power of a graph-based approach to capture a broad spectrum of genetic variation in S. cerevisiae, enabling accurate genotyping of both small and SVs at scale.

title: From genotype to phenotype with 1,086 near telomere-to-telomere yeast genomes
main_content: a construction based on the alignments of homologous chromosomes prevents the detection of reciprocal translocations.
context: the limitations of current graph-building algorithms in providing a truly exhaustive representation of genomic variants within a population
outcome: This underscores the relevance of an assembly-based pangenome approach.
